args.why: Stage 2 (architecture) needs a docs/SUBSYSTEMS.md that turns the SPEC requirements into a block-level architecture: one section per subsystem with reasoning and key values, holding every SPEC.md budget. This board is a passive USB-C 5V sink breakout, so the MCU and connectivity 'subsystems' are intentionally absent and that absence must be recorded so it doesn't read as an oversight.
args.what_changes:
- Add docs/SUBSYSTEMS.md with a prose block diagram and one section per subsystem: Input/USB-C connector, CC sink termination, Short-circuit protection, ESD/surge, Output distribution (screw terminal + header), Power-good LED (UI), Mechanical.
- Explicitly record that MCU and Connectivity subsystems do not exist on this board (no data, no negotiation) and why their absence is intentional.
- Carry every SPEC.md §3 budget (3A rating, 5V/6V, <2mA quiescent incl. LED split, board area, BOM, mounting) into the relevant subsystem section as its governing constraint.
args.tasks:
- [ ] Write docs/SUBSYSTEMS.md
- [ ] Record architecture decisions (no-MCU, no-connectivity, subsystem partitioning)
- [ ] check_drift (docs only; no schematic yet)
- [ ] finish

data.text:
```json
{"tool":"write_file","args":{"path":"docs/SUBSYSTEMS.md","content":"# SUBSYSTEMS: USB-C Power Breakout

Stage 2 — architecture. This document decomposes the SPEC (`docs/SPEC.md`)
into block-level subsystems, one section each, with reasoning and key
values. Every numeric budget in SPEC.md §3 is carried into the subsystem it
governs. No parts are selected here (that is the part-selection stage); this
is the block diagram and the constraints each block must meet.

## 1. Block diagram (in prose)

```
   USB-C source (charger/host)
           |
     [USB-C receptacle]  <-- VBUS, GND, CC1, CC2 (D+/D- unused)
        |        |
        |        +--> [CC sink termination: Rd on CC1, Rd on CC2 -> GND]
        |
      VBUS_IN
        |
     [ESD/surge: TVS VBUS->GND at the connector]
        |
     [Short-circuit protection device]  (VBUS_IN -> VBUS_OUT)
        |
      VBUS_OUT ------+-----------------+-------------------+
        |            |                 |                   |
   [Power-good     [Screw terminal   [2x2 0.1"          (GND plane
    LED + Rs        VBUS/GND]         header VBUS/GND]    common to all)
    -> GND]
```

Signal flow is one-directional: 5V enters at the USB-C receptacle, passes
through ESD clamping and the short-circuit protection device, and is
distributed to the two output connectors and the indicator LED. CC1/CC2
branch off at the connector purely to present the sink pull-downs; they
carry no power and no data. D+/D- are intentionally unconnected (SPEC §6).

There is **no MCU subsystem and no connectivity subsystem** — see §8.

## 2. Input / USB-C receptacle subsystem

**Role:** Accept 5V VBUS + GND from a USB-C cable, sink-side only. Expose
CC1/CC2 to the CC termination block. D+/D- not routed (SPEC §6).

**Governing budgets:**
- Input voltage: 5V nominal, must survive up to 6V (SPEC §3). All parts on
  the VBUS_IN node inherit the 6V survive rating as their minimum working
  voltage; part selection should pick ≥10V-rated caps/TVS for margin.
- Output current rating: 3A continuous (SPEC §3/§3.1). The receptacle's
  VBUS and GND power contacts must be rated ≥3A. This drives receptacle
  choice toward a part whose VBUS/GND pins (often doubled) carry 3A.
- Board area ≤30x20mm (SPEC §3): the receptacle dominates one edge; its
  footprint is the largest single mechanical consumer and constrains layout.

**Key architectural values:**
- Wired as a **sink** (UFP power role). Only VBUS, GND, CC1, CC2 used.
- CC1 and CC2 are brought out **separately**, each to its own Rd (see §3) —
  not tied together — so the board works in either plug orientation.

## 3. CC sink termination subsystem

**Role:** Make the board look like a legal USB-C 5V sink so a compliant
source turns VBUS on. Each CC pin terminates through a pull-down (Rd) to GND
(SPEC §2.3). No CC logic, no PD PHY, no e-marker.

**Governing budgets / rules:**
- Two separate Rd resistors, one on CC1 and one on CC2, each to GND. Using
  one shared resistor is wrong — it would misrepresent the termination when
  both CC lines are used and breaks orientation independence.
- The **Rd value** (nominally 5.1kΩ per the Type-C spec) and its full
  rationale are deferred to `docs/DECISIONS.md` at part selection, per SPEC
  §2.3 which flags this as the single detail most likely to be wrong. This
  document only fixes the topology: two pull-downs, CC1->GND and CC2->GND.
- Quiescent impact: with VBUS present the source's Rp + this Rd form a
  divider; the standing current through each Rd is sub-100µA at 5V and is
  part of the <2mA quiescent budget (SPEC §3.2, "everything else" ≤1mA
  slice). Comfortable margin.

## 4. Short-circuit protection subsystem

**Role:** Protect the output, connector, and upstream source against a
downstream short without destroying the board (SPEC §2.5).

**Governing budgets:**
- Must pass 3A continuous without tripping (SPEC §3 output rating). Trip
  threshold assumed 3.5–4A (SPEC §5, ASSUMED) — headroom above 3A so normal
  full-load use never nuisance-trips.
- Method assumed: **resettable / auto-recovering current limiter**
  (eFuse or PTC-style), not a one-time fuse (SPEC §5). A breadboard tool
  should recover after a transient short.
- Voltage: sits on the VBUS path, so rated for ≥6V survive (SPEC §3).
- **Quiescent draw is a hard constraint:** if an active device (eFuse) is
  chosen, its own supply/bias current counts against the ≤1mA "everything
  else" slice of the 2mA budget (SPEC §3.2). A PTC's leakage is negligible
  but its series resistance and I²t must still pass 3A. The specific device,
  its trip point, and its Iq are recorded in DECISIONS.md at part selection
  and must be checked against SPEC §3.2 there.
- Cost: contributes to the <$3 qty-100 BOM (SPEC §3). eFuse vs PTC is partly
  a cost/quiescent tradeoff resolved at part selection.

## 5. ESD / surge protection subsystem

**Role:** Clamp ESD/surge events entering at the user-facing USB-C
connector (SPEC §5, ASSUMED). One low-capacitance TVS across VBUS/GND at the
receptacle.

**Governing budgets:**
- Working voltage above 6V survive level, clamp below the ratings of
  downstream parts (SPEC §3).
- Placed at the connector, before the protection device, so surge energy is
  shunted at the entry point.
- Cost/area: a single small TVS; must fit the <$3 BOM (SPEC §3) — flagged in
  SPEC §5 for revisit if it conflicts with the cost target.
- No data lines to protect (D+/D- unused), so this is VBUS-only; no data-line
  ESD array is needed.

## 6. Output distribution subsystem

**Role:** Deliver VBUS_OUT/GND to the two user connectors (SPEC §2.2):
a 2-pin 3.5mm screw terminal and a 2x2 0.1" pin header.

**Governing budgets:**
- Both connectors and all copper between the protection device and them must
  carry **3A continuous** (SPEC §3/§3.1). Screw terminal: pick a 3.5mm part
  rated ≥3A. Header: 0.1" pins are typically rated a few amps each; VBUS and
  GND each get one pin of the 2x2 (the 2x2 = VBUS, VBUS? no) — topology is
  one VBUS pin pair and one GND pin pair, or VBUS/GND on each row; exact pin
  assignment fixed at schematic stage. The point here: the header must be
  wired so the 3A path has adequate pin/copper.
- "3A" is a **rating, not a negotiated guarantee** (SPEC §3.1) — silkscreen
  and any labels on these connectors must not imply PD or guaranteed 3A.
- Trace width / copper weight to hit 3A on 2-layer 1oz (SPEC §4) is a layout
  obligation, deferred to the layout stage.

## 7. UI / power-good LED subsystem

**Role:** A single visible LED that is on whenever VBUS is present (SPEC
§2.4). LED + series resistor from VBUS_OUT to GND.

**Governing budgets:**
- **LED branch current ≤1mA** (SPEC §3.2, ASSUMED split of the 2mA total).
  At ~5V this sets the series resistor: a modern high-efficiency LED is
  clearly visible at well under 1mA, so the series resistor is sized for
  a few hundred µA to stay safely inside the slice. Exact LED + resistor at
  part selection; the ≤1mA ceiling is the hard architectural value.
- Combined with the "everything else" ≤1mA slice, total quiescent stays
  under the 2mA hard budget (SPEC §3).
- Passive size ≥0603 (SPEC §4) for the resistor and LED.
- Indicates VBUS presence only — not "5V regulated" or "PD active"; there is
  no regulation or negotiation on this board.

## 8. Absent subsystems (intentional — not an oversight)

These blocks appear in the stage-2 template but **do not exist on this
board**, by design and per SPEC:

- **MCU / compute:** none. The board performs no logic, no CC reading, no
  current-limit enforcement (SPEC §1, §3.1). Adding an MCU would blow the
  <$3 BOM and the <2mA quiescent budget and is explicitly out of the brief.
- **Connectivity / data:** none. D+/D- are not routed to anything (SPEC §6).
  No USB data, no wireless, no PD communication PHY.
- **Power conversion / regulation:** none. Output is pass-through 5V VBUS;
  there is no buck/boost/LDO. "5V nominal" is whatever the source supplies
  (SPEC §3.1). No inductor, no switching node.
- **Negotiation (USB-PD/BC1.2):** none (SPEC §1, §6). Sink presents Rd only.

Recording their absence here prevents a later reviewer from reading a
missing MCU/regulator/PD chip as a mistake.

## 9. Budget traceability summary

| SPEC §3 budget | Owning subsystem(s) | How held |
|---|---|---|
| 3A continuous rating | Input (§2), Protection (§4), Output (§6) | Connector/device ratings ≥3A; trace width at layout |
| 5V nominal / 6V survive | Input (§2), ESD (§5), Protection (§4) | All VBUS-node parts rated ≥6V (≥10V chosen for margin) |
| <2mA quiescent (incl. LED) | LED (§7) ≤1mA + Protection/CC (§3,§4) ≤1mA | Split per SPEC §3.2; verified at part selection |
| Board area ≤30x20mm | Input (§2) + Output (§6) footprints | Layout stage |
| BOM <$3 @ qty100 | all | Part selection stage |
| Mounting: 2x M3, 24mm pitch | Mechanical | Layout stage |

## 10. Traceability

Source: `docs/SPEC.md` (all §3 budgets, §5 assumptions) and `brief.md`.
Items marked ASSUMED there (protection method/trip, TVS, quiescent split,
temperature) remain assumptions here and are resolved at part selection.
"}}
```

[result of record_decision]
Recorded to docs/DECISIONS.md.

args.decision: Architecture (stage 2) partitions the board into 7 real subsystems (USB-C input, CC sink termination, short-circuit protection, ESD/surge, output distribution, power-good LED/UI, mechanical) plus an explicit 'absent subsystems' section (no MCU, no connectivity, no regulation, no PD negotiation).
args.rationale: SPEC defines a passive 5V pass-through sink; recording MCU/connectivity/regulation absence in docs/SUBSYSTEMS.md §8 prevents a later reviewer reading a missing chip as an oversight, and keeps the <$3 BOM and <2mA quiescent budgets intact.

args.summary: Stage 2 architecture complete. Wrote docs/SUBSYSTEMS.md: prose block diagram plus one section per subsystem (USB-C input, CC sink termination, short-circuit protection, ESD/surge, output distribution, power-good LED/UI, mechanical), each carrying its governing SPEC §3 budget. §8 explicitly records the intentional absence of MCU, connectivity, power conversion, and PD-negotiation subsystems so it doesn't read as an oversight. §9 is a budget-to-subsystem traceability table holding all six SPEC budgets (3A rating, 5V/6V, <2mA quiescent with LED ≤1mA split, board area, BOM, mounting). No parts selected (deferred to part-selection); no schematic yet so no ERC/DRC/drift obligations. Decision recorded.

args.rationale: SPEC defines a passive 5V pass-through sink; recording MCU/connectivity/regulation absence in docs/SUBSYSTEMS.md §8 prevents a later reviewer reading a missing chip as an oversight, and keeps the <$3 BOM and <2mA quiescent budgets intact.
